sast_review.reason: The `filename` returned by `validate_filename` is only basename-checked but not sanitized against traversal. `os.path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, filename)` can still be exploited if `filename` is e.g. `../etc/passwd` because `os.path.join` will discard the prefix when an absolute path is used. No canonicalization or real-path check is performed.
The `filename` from `validate_filename` is used in an f-string path construction without any path sanitization or real-path check. An attacker can control the write location.
`os.system` invokes a shell, and `archive_name` and `filename` are embedded in the command string without escaping. This allows command injection.

The `filename` from the multipart upload is used directly in `os.path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, filename)` without any validation beyond the extension check. `os.path.join` does not prevent traversal; an attacker can upload to arbitrary paths.

raw.summary: The code has multiple critical security issues: path traversal in file reads (line 18, line 35, line 57), OS command injection via os.system (line 37), and HTML injection in the response (line 66). The SAST findings are all true positives; no effective sanitization or safe API usage is present.
